chunk_summary: This section discusses inpatient stabilization and antibiotic regimens for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) when it coexists with stunting, focusing on WHO guidelines and facility-based care protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of antibiotics in managing complicated SAM with stunting.
Learn the key steps for inpatient stabilization of children with SAM.
Familiarize with WHO and national guidelines for treating SAM in healthcare facilities.
Identify complications associated with SAM and their management strategies.
key_facts: WHO recommends routine antibiotics for children with complicated SAM to prevent infections.
Amoxicillin is commonly used as a first-line antibiotic for SAM cases, with dosage based on weight and age.
Inpatient stabilization includes managing hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and dehydration before initiating nutritional rehabilitation.
Complicated SAM is defined by the presence of medical complications such as infections, severe edema, or failure of outpatient care.
Facility-based care protocols emphasize monitoring vital signs, fluid management, and gradual refeeding to avoid refeeding syndrome.
reference_quote: WHO recommends routine antibiotic therapy for children with severe acute malnutrition to reduce the risk of infections and improve survival rates. (Source: https://www.who.int/tools/elena/interventions/antibiotics-sam)
document_key_topics: WHO and national guidelines for SAM management
Complications of SAM and their treatment
